1:
"Then the LORD said unto Moses, Now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh: for with a strong hand shall he let them go, and with a strong hand shall he drive them out of his land." The witnesses note God's answer to Moses' complaint. On "Now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh," the grammatical voices note the assurance. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh" — God answered Moses' complaint (Exodus 5:22-23) with the assurance that he would now act; the promise of the deliverance about to begin; the comfort to the perplexed servant (the deliverance delayed, not denied); God's answer to the trial of faith.

On "with a strong hand shall he let them go," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the power. Calvin and JFB note "with a strong hand" — by God's mighty power (the plagues, the strong hand of God on Pharaoh, Exodus 3:19-20); the deliverance to be wrought by God's almighty hand; Pharaoh forced to let them go.

On "with a strong hand shall he drive them out of his land," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the issue: "with a strong hand shall he drive them out of his land" — Pharaoh, so far from refusing, would be made to drive Israel out (the issue of the plagues, Exodus 12:31-33); the complete reversal (the king who would not let them go now thrusting them out); the providence (God's strong hand overruling the proud king). The verse displays God's answer to Moses' complaint.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the assurance — "Now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh," the answer (Exodus 5:22, Henry, Gill); "with a strong hand shall he let them go," the power (Exodus 3:19, Calvin, JFB); "drive them out of his land," the issue (Exodus 12:31, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the answering God. The verse marks the answer: now shalt thou see. The verse displays the strong hand: of God. The verse displays the Pharaoh: forced. The verse displays the deliverance: at hand. The answer displays God assuring the deliverance. The verse displays the God: promising. The verse displays the Pharaoh: to drive them out. The verse displays the comfort: to Moses. God's answer to Moses' complaint displays the assurance: now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh, with a strong hand (Exodus 3:19-20; 12:31) — the deliverance about to begin by God's mighty hand, the answer to the trial of faith. The verse displays the self-revealing God (vv.2-3).
2:
"And God spake unto Moses, and said unto him, I am the LORD:" The witnesses note God's self-revelation as the LORD. On "God spake unto Moses, and said unto him, I am the LORD," the grammatical voices note the declaration. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "God spake unto Moses, and said unto him, I am the LORD (Jehovah)" — God prefaced his renewed promise with the great declaration "I am the LORD" (Jehovah, the covenant Name, the self-existent, eternal, covenant-keeping God); the ground of all that follows; the Name that answers Pharaoh's "Who is the LORD?" (Exodus 5:2); the foundation of the covenant assurance.

On "I am the LORD," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the Name. Calvin and Wesley note "I am the LORD" (Jehovah, Exodus 3:14-15, "I AM THAT I AM") — the Name of God's self-existence, eternity, and covenant faithfulness; the ground of the promises (vv.6-8, each sealed with "I am the LORD"); the assurance that the unchanging God would keep his covenant.

On the declaration, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the foundation: "I am the LORD" — the foundation of the whole renewed promise (the faithful, covenant God speaking); the answer to Moses' perplexity (the LORD himself the surety of the deliverance); the Name on which the redemption rests (the eternal, covenant-keeping God). The verse displays God's self-revelation as the LORD.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the declaration — "I am the LORD," the covenant Name (Exodus 5:2, Keil, Gill); "I am the LORD," Jehovah (Exodus 3:14, Calvin, Wesley); the foundation (vv.6-8, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the self-revealing God. The verse marks the declaration: I am the LORD. The verse displays the Name: Jehovah. The verse displays the covenant: the ground. The verse displays the assurance: founded. The declaration displays God revealing himself as the LORD. The verse displays the God: speaking. The verse displays the Name: Jehovah. The verse displays the promise: grounded. The verse displays the God known to the fathers (v.3).
3:
"And I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty, but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them." The witnesses note the Name not known to the patriarchs — the crux of the divine Name. On "I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty," the grammatical voices note the patriarchs. Ellicott and Gill note that "I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty (El Shaddai)" — God had revealed himself to the patriarchs chiefly as "God Almighty" (El Shaddai, the God able to fulfill his promises, Genesis 17:1; 28:3; 35:11); the name of the covenant-making God of promise; the God who pledged the promises to the fathers.

On "but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them," interpreters weigh the crux. The reformed and the dominant reading note the difficulty — for the name Jehovah was certainly in use long before (Genesis 4:26; 15:7; 22:14; the patriarchs used it); so "was I not known" cannot mean they had never heard or used the name. The readings: (a) the dominant — God was not "known" to the patriarchs by the name Jehovah in its full meaning and experience: that is, they knew the name, but did not know (experience) God as Jehovah in the sense now to be displayed — the covenant-keeping God actually fulfilling his promises (the patriarchs received the promises by faith, but did not see them performed; now God would make himself "known" as Jehovah, the faithful performer of the covenant, in the redemption from Egypt, the name "known" by the experience of its fulfillment, vv.6-8); (b) some take it that the full revelation of the name's import (the self-existent, covenant-fulfilling God) was now first given; (c) a minority read it as a question ("by my name Jehovah was I not known to them?"); the witnesses agree the substance: the patriarchs knew God as El Shaddai, the God of promise; now Israel would know him as Jehovah, the God who performs the promise, the covenant-keeper made known in the act of redemption.

On the Name, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the fulfillment: "by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them" — the name Jehovah (the faithful, covenant-performing God) now to be "known" by the experience of the deliverance (the promises to the fathers now fulfilled); the patriarchs had the promise, the children would have the performance; the name made known in the act. The verse displays the Name not known to the patriarchs.

The witnesses agree the substance: God appeared to the patriarchs as El Shaddai (God Almighty, the God of promise), but was not "known" to them by the name Jehovah in the sense now to be displayed (Ellicott, Gill, Clarke). The phrase bears its weight as the crux of the Name: not that the patriarchs never heard the name (it was in use, Genesis 4:26; 15:7), but that they did not "know" (experience) God as Jehovah — the covenant-keeping God actually fulfilling his promises; they had the promise, Israel would have the performance; the name made known by the experience of the redemption (vv.6-8). The verse displays the divine-Name crux. The Name displays God known by the experience of the redemption. The verse displays the fathers: the God of promise. The verse displays the children: the God who performs. The Name not known to the patriarchs displays the crux: by El Shaddai I appeared to the fathers, but by Jehovah was I not known (Genesis 17:1; 4:26; 15:7) — the patriarchs knew the God of promise, Israel would know the God who performs it, the name made known by the experience of the redemption. The verse displays the covenant remembered (vv.4-5).
4:
"And I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers." The witnesses note God's covenant to give Canaan to the fathers. On "I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan," the grammatical voices note the covenant. Ellicott and Gill note that "I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their pilgrimage" — God recalled the covenant he had established with the patriarchs (Genesis 15:18; 17:8; 26:3; 28:13), to give them the land of Canaan; the covenant of the land; the ground of the deliverance (God bringing Israel to the promised land, in keeping with the oath to the fathers).

On "the land of Canaan," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the promise. Calvin and Gill note the land of Canaan (the promised inheritance, Genesis 17:8) — the covenant land; the goal of the deliverance; the oath to the fathers to be fulfilled.

On "the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers," the grammatical-historical note the pilgrimage: "the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers" — the patriarchs sojourned in Canaan as strangers (Genesis 23:4; Hebrews 11:9, 13), holding only a burial-place, looking for the inheritance; now God would give the land to their seed (the covenant fulfilled); the pilgrim fathers' hope to be realized in their children. The verse displays God's covenant to give Canaan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the covenant — "I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan," the covenant of the land (Genesis 17:8, Ellicott, Gill); "the land of Canaan," the promise (Calvin, Gill); "the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers," the pilgrimage (Hebrews 11:9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covenant God. The verse marks the covenant: established. The verse displays the land: Canaan. The verse displays the pilgrimage: of the fathers. The verse displays the promise: to be fulfilled. The covenant displays God's oath of the land. The verse displays the God: covenanting. The verse displays the land: promised. The verse displays the fathers: pilgrims. God's covenant to give Canaan displays the oath to the fathers: I established my covenant to give them the land of their pilgrimage (Genesis 17:8; Hebrews 11:9, 13) — the pilgrim fathers' hope, the inheritance to be given their seed. The verse displays the groaning heard (v.5).
5:
"And I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel, whom the Egyptians keep in bondage; and I have remembered my covenant." The witnesses note God hearing the groaning and remembering his covenant. On "I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel," the grammatical voices note the hearing. Gill and Wesley note that "I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel, whom the Egyptians keep in bondage" — God had heard Israel's groaning under the Egyptian bondage (Exodus 2:23-24); the cry of the oppressed reaching God; the compassion of God toward his afflicted people; the ground of the deliverance (God moved by their groaning and his covenant).

On "whom the Egyptians keep in bondage," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the bondage. Calvin and Gill note the bondage (the cruel servitude, Exodus 5) — the affliction God regarded; the oppression that moved his compassion and faithfulness.

On "I have remembered my covenant," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the remembrance: "I have remembered my covenant" — God remembering his covenant with the fathers (Exodus 2:24; Genesis 15:13-18); not that he had forgotten, but now acting on it; the covenant the ground of the deliverance (God redeeming for his oath, not Israel's worth); the faithfulness moving to redeem. The verse displays God hearing the groaning and remembering his covenant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the hearing — "I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel," the cry heard (Exodus 2:23, Gill, Wesley); "whom the Egyptians keep in bondage," the bondage (Calvin, Gill); "I have remembered my covenant," the remembrance (Exodus 2:24; Genesis 15:13, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the remembering God. The verse marks the groaning: heard. The verse displays the bondage: regarded. The verse displays the covenant: remembered. 6:
"Wherefore say unto the children of Israel, I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will rid you out of their bondage, and I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments:" The witnesses note the sevenfold "I will" of redemption begun — the crux of the covenant promises. On "I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians," the grammatical voices note the promises. Ellicott and Gill note that "I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens... I will rid you out of their bondage... I will redeem you with a stretched out arm" — God, sealing each promise with "I am the LORD," made the great series of "I will" promises of redemption (the sevenfold "I will" of vv.6-8): I will bring you out, I will rid (deliver), I will redeem, I will take you for a people, I will be your God, I will bring you in, I will give you the land; the covenant promises of the full redemption; the certainty (grounded on "I am the LORD").

On "I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments," interpreters note the redemption. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the redemption — "I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments": God promised to redeem (the word of buying back, ransoming) Israel by his outstretched arm (his almighty power) and great judgments (the plagues on Egypt, Exodus 7-12; the judgment on the Red Sea); the redemption by power and judgment (a type of the great redemption by Christ, the people bought out of bondage); the three acts of v.6 (bring out, rid, redeem) — the deliverance from Egypt's bondage by God's mighty hand.

On the promises, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the certainty: the "I will" promises (each sure, grounded on "I am the LORD") — the answer to Moses' complaint and the people's despair (the certain pledge of the redemption); the covenant of grace foreshadowed (God redeeming his people, taking them for his own); the sevenfold assurance. The verse displays the sevenfold "I will" of redemption begun.

The witnesses agree the substance: God, sealing all with "I am the LORD," promised to bring out, rid, and redeem Israel by his outstretched arm and great judgments (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the covenant promises: the sevenfold "I will" (vv.6-8) — bring out, rid, redeem, take for a people, be their God, bring in, give the land — the great series of redemption promises, each grounded on "I am the LORD," the certain pledge of the full deliverance; "I will redeem you with a stretched out arm and great judgments" (the redemption by God's power and the plagues, a type of the redemption by Christ). The verse displays the redemption-promises crux. The verse marks the LORD: the ground. The verse displays the I will: sevenfold. The verse displays the redeem: with a stretched arm. The verse displays the judgments: great. The promises display God's certain pledge of redemption. The verse displays the God: promising. The verse displays the redemption: assured. The verse displays the arm: stretched out. The sevenfold "I will" of redemption begun displays the crux: I am the LORD, I will bring you out, rid you, redeem you with a stretched out arm and great judgments (Exodus 7-12) — the certain covenant pledge of the full redemption, grounded on the Name, a type of the redemption by Christ. The verse displays the covenant relation (v.7).
7:
"And I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God: and ye shall know that I am the LORD your God, which bringeth you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians." The witnesses note the covenant relation — "I will be to you a God" — the crux of the covenant formula. On "I will take you to me for a people," interpreters expound the covenant. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the great promise — "I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God": God promised to take Israel as his own people and to be their God; the covenant formula (the heart of the covenant, repeated through Scripture, Genesis 17:7; Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 6:16; Revelation 21:3, "they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God"); the gracious relation (God taking a poor, enslaved, unworthy people to be his own — the witnesses marvel: God taking the worst of bondmen to be his peculiar people, of his free grace, not their worth); the covenant of grace (the redeemed taken to be God's people, and God their God — the very essence of the covenant, fulfilled in Christ and the church).

On "I will be to you a God," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the relation. Wesley and Gill note "I will be to you a God" — God giving himself to be Israel's God (the highest blessing, God himself their portion, Psalm 144:15; the covenant relation, all that "God" means pledged to them); the grace of the covenant (God their God, they his people).

On "ye shall know that I am the LORD your God, which bringeth you out," the grammatical-historical note the knowledge: "ye shall know that I am the LORD your God, which bringeth you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians" — Israel would know (by the experience of the deliverance) that the LORD is their God (the name Jehovah known by the redemption, v.3); the knowledge grounded in the act (the deliverance proving God their covenant God); the covenant sealed by the experience of redemption. The verse displays the covenant relation.

The witnesses agree the substance: God promised to take Israel for his people and to be their God, that they might know him as the LORD their God (Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the covenant formula: "I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God" — the heart of the covenant (repeated through Scripture, Genesis 17:7; Jeremiah 31:33; Revelation 21:3), God of his free grace taking a poor, enslaved, unworthy people to be his own and giving himself to be their God; the covenant of grace, fulfilled in Christ and the church; the relation known by the experience of the redemption (v.3). The verse displays the covenant-formula crux. The verse marks the people: taken by God. The verse displays the God: their God. The verse displays the covenant: the formula. The verse displays the grace: free. The relation displays God taking Israel for his own people. The verse displays the God: covenanting. The verse displays the people: his own. The verse displays the grace: marvelous. The verse displays the promised land (v.8).
8:
"And I will bring you in unto the land, concerning the which I did swear to give it to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it you for an heritage: I am the LORD." The witnesses note the promise of the land sealed with "I am the LORD." On "I will bring you in unto the land, concerning the which I did swear," the grammatical voices note the land. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "I will bring you in unto the land, concerning the which I did swear to give it to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it you for an heritage" — God completed the sevenfold "I will" with the promise to bring Israel into the land sworn to the fathers (Genesis 15:18; 17:8; 26:3; 28:13), and to give it for an inheritance; the goal of the redemption (the promised land); the oath to the fathers fulfilled.

On "I will give it you for an heritage," the reformed and witnesses note the inheritance. Gill notes the land given "for an heritage" (a permanent possession, the inheritance of the covenant people) — the goal of the deliverance; the promised inheritance; the covenant fulfilled (the pilgrim fathers' hope, v.4, realized in their seed).

On "I am the LORD," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the seal: "I am the LORD" — the sevenfold promise sealed (as begun, v.6) with "I am the LORD" (the covenant Name the surety of all); the certainty (the faithful, covenant God the guarantee of every promise); the assurance grounded on the Name; the redemption pledged by the LORD himself. The verse displays the promise of the land sealed with "I am the LORD."

The verse displays the unhearkening people (v.9).
9:
"And Moses spake so unto the children of Israel: but they hearkened not unto Moses for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage." The witnesses note the people not hearkening for anguish and bondage — the crux of the crushed spirit. On "Moses spake so unto the children of Israel: but they hearkened not unto Moses," the grammatical voices note the failure. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses spake so unto the children of Israel: but they hearkened not unto Moses" — Moses delivered God's glorious promises (vv.6-8) to the people, but they did not hearken (would not, or could not, receive them); the sad failure of faith (the people, lately believing, Exodus 4:31, now unable to believe); the contrast (the rich promises met with unbelief).

On "for anguish of spirit," interpreters expound the cause. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the cause — "for anguish of spirit (Hebrew, shortness of spirit, impatience), and for cruel bondage": the people did not hearken because of their anguish of spirit (their crushed, impatient, despairing state) and their cruel bondage (the harder servitude, Exodus 5); the witnesses note the cause with sympathy: not mere willful unbelief, but the crushing of their spirit under the affliction (the soul so broken by suffering that it could not rise to faith and hope); the deadening effect of long, cruel oppression (despair choking the promise); yet also a measure of unbelief and impatience (the weakness of faith under trial, the people not laying hold of God's word); the lesson — affliction may so crush the spirit that even God's promises are not received (the danger of despair under prolonged suffering), and the need of grace to believe when the heart is broken.

On "and for cruel bondage," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the bondage: "and for cruel bondage" — the hard servitude (Exodus 5:6-19) that crushed their spirit; the affliction that choked their faith; the providence (the people too broken to believe, that the deliverance might be wholly of God, not of their faith or works); the trial that prepared them to be saved by grace alone. The verse displays the people not hearkening for anguish and bondage.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses spoke the promises, but the people hearkened not, for anguish of spirit and cruel bondage (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the crushed spirit: "they hearkened not... for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage" — not mere willful unbelief, but the crushing of their spirit under the cruel affliction (despair choking the promise, the soul too broken by suffering to rise to faith); yet a measure of the weakness of faith under trial; the lesson that affliction may so crush the spirit that even God's promises are not received, the need of grace to believe when the heart is broken, the deliverance wholly of God. The verse displays the crushed-spirit crux. The verse marks the promises: spoken. The verse displays the failure: hearkened not. The verse displays the anguish: of spirit. The verse displays the bondage: cruel. The failure displays the people too crushed to believe. The verse displays the Moses: speaking. The verse displays the people: despairing. The verse displays the faith: choked. The verse displays the renewed charge to Moses (vv.10-13).

11:
"Go in, speak unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land." The very brief verse notes the renewed charge to demand Israel's release. The grammatical voices note the command. Ellicott and Gill note that "Go in, speak unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land" — the LORD charged Moses to go again to Pharaoh and demand Israel's release; the renewed demand (as Exodus 5:1); the persistence of God's purpose (the demand pressed despite Pharaoh's refusal and the people's despair); the commission renewed.

12:
"And Moses spake before the LORD, saying, Behold, the children of Israel have not hearkened unto me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me, who am of uncircumcised lips?" The witnesses note Moses' objection of his uncircumcised lips. On "the children of Israel have not hearkened unto me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me," the grammatical voices note the objection. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses spake before the LORD... Behold, the children of Israel have not hearkened unto me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me?" — Moses objected: if his own people, with the glorious promises, would not hearken (v.9), how should Pharaoh, the proud enemy, hear him? — the argument from the lesser to the greater; the discouragement of Moses (his faith weak after the failure); the objection raised.
